label: ADB project appraisal parameters (carbon, shadow pricing)

carbon_values.description: ADB adopted shadow carbon pricing aligned with the multilateral development bank (MDB) joint approach. Values are consistent with the World Bank schedule.
carbon_values.note: Same as World Bank schedule. Part of the MDB joint approach to carbon pricing adopted in 2017.
carbon_values.source_detail: MDB Joint Approach to Paris Alignment and Shadow Carbon Pricing

shadow_exchange_rate.description: Shadow Exchange Rate Factor (SERF) for ADB Developing Member Countries. Same methodology as World Bank.
shadow_exchange_rate.source_detail: ADB Shadow Exchange Rates Technical Note (2015)

standard_conversion_factor.description: Standard Conversion Factor (SCF) = 1 / SERF. Applied to non-traded goods.

shadow_wage_rate.description: Shadow Wage Rate Factor for unskilled labour in ADB Developing Member Countries.

urban_formal.note: Urban areas with moderate unemployment. Workers have alternative informal sector employment.

rural_agricultural.note: Rural areas where displaced workers return to subsistence agriculture. Opportunity cost is the marginal product of agricultural labour.

severe_underemployment.note: Remote areas, post-disaster, or fragile states with severe labour surplus.

skilled_labour.note: Professional and skilled labour always valued at market wage.

shadow_wage_rate.note: ADB recommends country-specific calculation based on local labour market conditions. These are indicative starting points.

vsl.description: ADB does not publish a single VSL figure. Recommends the benefit-transfer method using World Bank income-group values or the OECD income-elasticity approach.
vsl.reference: Use the World Bank income-group VSL values from wb/appraisal-parameters.json, or the OECD transfer method from oecd/vsl-transfer.json.

source.publication: Guidelines for the Economic Analysis of Projects (2017); Shadow Exchange Rates Technical Note (2015); MDB Joint Carbon Pricing Approach

caveats: ADB appraisal methodology is closely aligned with the World Bank. Many parameter values are shared.
Shadow pricing is critical for ADB projects. Market prices in developing Asia can significantly misstate economic values.
Country-specific SERF and SWRF should be calculated for each project. The typical values here are starting points.
ADB regional examples for SWRF are approximate and depend on local conditions.
The 2017 Guidelines are the latest comprehensive version. Supplementary notes have been published since.
